coredata.dc:title: Recent development of gold nanochips in biosensing and biodiagnosis sensibilization strategies in vitro based on SPR, SERS and FRET optical properties

coredata.dc:description: Gold nanomaterials have become attractive nanomaterials for biomedical research due to their unique physical and chemical properties, and nanochips are designed to manufacture high-quality substrates for loading gold nanoparticles (GNPs) to achieve specific and selective detection. By utilizing multiple optical properties of different gold nanostructures, the sensitivity, specificity, speed, contrast, resolution, and other performance of biosensing and biological diagnosis can be significantly improved. This paper summarized the sensitivity enhancement strategies of optical biosensing techniques based on the three main optical properties of gold nanomaterials: surface plasmon resonance (SPR), surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). The aim is to comprehensively review the development direction of in vitro diagnostics (IVDs) from two aspects: detection strategies and modification of gold nanomaterials. In addition, some opportunities and challenges that gold-based IVDs may encounter at present or in the future are also mentioned in this paper. In summary, this paper can enlighten readers with feasible strategies for manufacturing potential gold-based nanobiosensors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Gold nanomaterials have become attractive nanomaterials for biomedical research due to their unique physical and chemical properties, and nanochips are designed to manufacture high-quality substrates for loading gold nanoparticles (GNPs) to achieve specific and selective detection. By utilizing multiple optical properties of different gold nanostructures, the sensitivity, specificity, speed, contrast, resolution, and other performance of biosensing and biological diagnosis can be significantly improved. This paper summarized the sensitivity enhancement strategies of optical biosensing techniques based on the three main optical properties of gold nanomaterials: surface plasmon resonance (SPR), surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). The aim is to comprehensively review the development direction of in vitro diagnostics (IVDs) from two aspects: detection strategies and modification of gold nanomaterials. In addition, some opportunities and challenges that gold-based IVDs may encounter at present or in the future are also mentioned in this paper. In summary, this paper can enlighten readers with feasible strategies for manufacturing potential gold-based nanobiosensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With the progress of nanotechnology, people pay more attention to the application of inorganic nanoparticles in various technologies, especially in biomedicine [1]. Detecting biomarkers are very important for early disease diagnosis, monitoring disease progress, and tracking the effectiveness of treatment intervention [2]. Timely and accurate detection can achieve the early intervention of the disease in order to reduce late medical expenditure [3,4]. The signal amplification effect of the biosensor based on inorganic nanoparticles directly affects the output efficiency of the results in vitro diagnostic testing [5,6]. It is worth noting that high detection sensitivity is vital for many critical applications, such as early detection of cancer and infectious diseases, and the concentrations of related biomarkers are deficient [7,8]. The various properties of biomarkers require a variety of biosensors to adapt to each biomarker, which is easier, faster, more sensitive, and more specific.

Similarly, Gold nanoparticles (GNPs) is an exciting material at any time because of their unique physical and chemical properties, such as optical adsorption of specific wavelengths of light, high electrical conductivity of rich surface electrons, and accessible surface modification with sulfhydryl groups. GNPs have different shapes and compositions at its nanoscale [9], their nano size is within the typical size range of biomolecules and organelles, which gives them unique physical and chemical properties [10]. Furthermore, GNPs allow almost one-to-one interaction with interested biomolecules [11]. Biosensors based on gold nanomaterials have various signal output functions, providing ideal properties and solid signals for chemical and biological detection, such as significant ratio of body surface area, optical properties given by localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) [12], wide absorption in the visible region of the electromagnetic spectrum [13], fine adjustable surface chemistry, and the broad compatibility of the structure [14,15]. Therefore, it can carry a variety of signal sensors (such as enzymes, DNA, and organic dyes). These properties promote the application of gold nanomaterials in developing of various hybrid systems for biosensors and molecular diagnostics. The local environment of gold nanomaterials is very sensitive to external stimuli, it is significant to make high-quality substrates to load GNPs to achieve specific and selective detection while minimizing background signal noise. In this era of rapid detection, accuracy and sensitivity are crucial requirements in biosensors, and gold nanochips can achieve signal amplification through a series of operations such as aggregation and fixation. So far, various gold nanochips-based biosensors have been developed for in vitro diagnostics (IVDs). The further development of diagnostic technology based on gold nanomaterials will provide modern clinical laboratories with unique analytical methods with higher sensitivity and multiplexing ability.

Despite numerous reports on gold nanoparticles, there is currently no systematic literature summarizing their applications in biological detection and imaging. In this paper, we will focus on the sensitivity enhancement strategies of optical biosensors and biological imaging techniques based on the three main optical properties of gold nanochip-based nanomaterials: surface plasmon resonance (SPR) [16], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [17] and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [18], and introduce some original gold-based nanobiosensors. Table 1
summarizes the specific diagnostic applications of gold nanomaterials. In addition, other forms of gold nanomaterials also can be used as highly sensitive biosensors. SPR (Surface Plasmon Resonance), SERS (Surface Enhanced Raman Scattering), and FRET (Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer) are three commonly used spectroscopic techniques in the fields of nanoscience and bioanalysis, each with unique principles and applications, as well as certain connections and complementarity. SPR technology is commonly used in fields such as biosensing and drug screening, monitoring the interactions between biomolecules by detecting changes in the refractive index of metal surfaces SERS has wide applications in material identification, structural characterization, biosensing, and other fields. The distance dependence of FRET makes it a powerful biological analysis tool for studying the distance and interactions between biomolecules. In the fields of biological analysis and sensing, SERS, SPR, and FRET can complement each other and provide multidimensional information. For example, SERS provides molecular vibration and structural information, SPR provides information on changes in interfacial refractive index, and FRET provides dynamic information on intermolecular distances and interactions. Although these techniques have their own characteristics, they all involve the interaction between light and matter, especially the behavior of light at the nanoscale (see Table 2).

Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) technology is a novel analytical technique developed based on optical principles. It refers to the resonance phenomenon that may occur when light undergoes total reflection on the surface of a prism and a metal film, forming an evanescent wave that enters the optically sparse medium, and there is a certain plasma wave in the medium (assuming it is a precious metal), and the two bands meet under the premise of energy conservation. This technology can detect the interactions between antigens and antibodies, DNA and proteins, and DNA and DNA, and has been widely used in fields such as life sciences, medical diagnosis, food safety, and environmental testing.

SPR is a biosensing analysis technology that utilizes traditional optical phenomena to generate evanescent waves in different media and resonate with plasma waves, thereby constructing the interaction between biomolecules. It is used to detect the interaction between ligands and analytes on biosensing chips. The light source of SPR biosensor is polarized light, and the sensing chip surface is coated with a layer of gold film. During the experiment, a biomolecule (target molecule) is first fixed on the surface of the gold film, and then the molecules that interact with it are dissolved in a solution (or mixed solution) and flowed over the chip surface. During the dissociation process between proteins on the gold film chip and molecules in the flow path, the resonance angle (i.e. SPR angle) will change accordingly. The detector detects this change, and based on the curve of this change, the binding constant Ka, dissociation constant K d, or affinity constant KD between molecules can be obtained through graphical analysis. The core components of the SPR measurement system include the optical system, sensor chip, liquid processing system, and other components.

For SPR biosensors, improving the sensitivity (signal-to-noise ratio) of SPR sensors is one of the essential problems. There are two ways to improve sensitivity: one is to reduce the noise, and the other is to increase the signal. Generally speaking, noise is caused by the non-specific binding of proteins to the sensors surface. Through a new method of preparing protein-based hydrogel, Gabinet et al. [44] proposed a new type of protein microarray, namely three-dimensional nanostructure protein hydrogel (3DNPH), which was prepared by distributing a small amount of protein-polymer mixture solution on the substrate. The crosslinking time of the distributed solution is very short before drying, and the resulting 3DNPH has a loose cross-linked thin sponge structure. Therefore, compared with the extensive volume system used in SPR protein microarray, a significant increase in the reaction ratio between ligands and analytes in this system can limit the reduction of non-specific binding to reduce noise.

However, for most researchers, increasing SPR signal strength is a more attractive option. Because the SPR optical properties of precious metal nanomaterials are highly related to the morphology, size, composition, dielectric environment, and distance between adjacent particles [45], the refractive index of nanoparticles is primarily affected by their structures. If nanostructures with different forms are used, a unique SPR response can be obtained. Springer et al. [46] proposed a theoretical model in which the signal enhancement produced by GNPs is related to its composition, size, and concentration, so that the response of SPR sensors affected by various GNPs can be accurately predicted. Based on this, the structural modification of gold nanomaterials to improve the sensitivity of SPR sensors has become an effective strategy (Fig. 1 ).

As the local environment of gold nanomaterials is very sensitive to external stimuli, it is significant to make high-quality substrates to load GNPs to achieve specific and selective detection while minimizing background signal noise. The ideal matrix should be durable enough to immobilize GNPs and subsequent chemical reactions. The nanometer-thick gold film on the glass substrate forms an excellent interface and has become the standard of plasmon excitation and chemical stability in SPR applications [58]. Laboratory on Chip (LOC) is one of the famous platforms with a high success rate of sensing technology [59]. Hsu, C. Y. et al. [60] reported a substrate with constant spacing for junction alloy nanostructure. The GNP with the average particle size of 8 nm was embedded into the glass substrate to obtain the uniform intergranular gap of 11 nm. A single IgG molecule is occupied separately under spatial constraints to capture anti-IgG. The apparent absorbance and wavelength redshift increase can be clearly observed when the combined event occurs. This phenomenon can be attributed to the rise of local refractive index in the gap region. In addition, the platform has excellent sensitivity to analytes, and the LOD of IgG is 66.7 pM. The system is then integrated with microfluidics, allowing LSPR analysis to achieve high-throughput and unlabeled detection [36].

Most of the reported unlabeled in rapid direct detection have been proven to be used to analyze target analyte [61,62] in buffer or diluted serum or plasma. These results are only a step forward in direct testing in minimal or untreated whole blood. To reduce the non-specific deposition of molecules, cells, and aggregates in the blood on the surface of biosensors [63], SPR sensor chip with an antifouling polymer brush structure and incorporated with thrombin aptamer bioreceptors was designed to address this challenge (Fig. 2 a). Poly [(N-(2-hydroxypropyl)-methacrylamide)-co-(carboxybetaine methacrylamide)] (poly (HPMA-co-CBMAA)) brushes were synthesized on the surface of gold sensor chip via photoinduced single-electron transfer living radical polymerization and postmodified with three thrombin aptamers (HD1 short, HD1 and HD22) (Fig. 2b). The affinity interactions of aptamer receptors with thrombin (and other molecules present in the blood) were studied, and changes in their performance when incorporated into polymer brushes were characterized. The combination of brushes and aptamer bioreceptors allows for the analysis of medically relevant concentrations of thrombin in the 10 % blood by direct SPR detection format (Fig. 2c). This is the first time that optical affinity biosensors can be used for the unlabeled analysis of biomarkers in minimally treated human blood without pre-separation steps.

The size of nanoparticles also plays a decisive role in LSPR displacement and has a significant influence on the sensitivity of LSPR sensors. Different sizes of GNPs were fixed on the glass substrate to detect michigan cancer foundation-7 (MCF-7) and RPE-1 cells [64]. The results show that compared with small-size GNPs, large-size GNPs have longer electromagnetic field attenuation lengths, providing a wider linear range and higher sensitivity. For example, the detection range of MCF-7 cells is 8.56 × 103 to 1.09 × 106 cells/mL on 500 nm NPs and 3.43 × 104 to 2.73 × 105 cells/mL on 60 nm GNPs. In addition, the larger GNPs showed higher cell capture efficiency than the smaller GNPs, thus showing a more significant LSPR shift. Gold-doped nanocomposites are increasingly essential structures for creating highly sensitive LSPR bioassay. These analyses take advantage of the benefits of the elements in the nanostructure.

Although SPR has analytical capabilities, due to the complexity of optical instruments and the need for accurate temperature control, SPR is not widely used in clinical immunoassay or other applications. The photothermal effect caused by plasmon resonance has an obvious influence on the analytical ability of SPR. However, due to the limitations of experimental methods, there are few studies on the theoretical mechanism and specific effects of SPR sensors with photothermal effect. Pang, J. et al. [65] used a multi-scale simulation method to study the photothermal effect of graphene/Au nano-interface. The quantitative contribution of the photothermal effect to high-reliability SPR sensors is calculated by first-principles calculation and molecular dynamics simulation to clarify the influence of the photothermal effect on sensing performance. The results show that because the dielectric constant of Au and graphene thin films can be adjusted by temperature, the sensitivity and detection accuracy of graphene/Au SPR sensors can be customized from 0 K to 600 K. By controlling the material thickness, interface combination and lattice strain, the optimized graphene/Au SPR sensor has higher sensitivity, detection accuracy, and reliability to temperature rise. This multi-scale simulation method can not only find the mechanism of interface phenomena but also provide good guidance for the performance optimization and commercial application of SPR sensors in analytical chemistry and biomedicine.

Some research work has been reported to improve the sensing performance of SPR biosensors [66,67] by using different materials on metal layers, such as Si, ZnO, TiO2, etc. Various types of 2D materials improve the sensing ability of SPR.

Natan and colleagues previously demonstrated that colloidal gold-enhanced SPR sandwich immunoassay is highly sensitive [68–70]. Based on this principle, Dang, T. et al. [71] showed a unique Au–TiO2–Au nanocup array chip sensor and skillfully used colloidal gold-labeled secondary antibodies with antibody-target protein-antibody sandwich method for dynamic detection, which improved the sensitivity and specificity. It is applied to the dynamic measurement of the binding kinetics of CRP and anti-CRP antibodies, which is sensitive enough in a wide concentration range. After that, Huang, L. et al. [72] also developed a novel nanoplasmonic immunosorbent assay (NanoPISA) platform for one-step rapid quantification of SARS-CoV-2 neutralizing antibodies (NAs) in clinical serum samples for high-throughput evaluation of COVID-19 vaccine effectiveness. By using the NanoPISA platform enhanced by the coupling of nanoporous hollow GNPs, SARS-CoV-2NAs can be detected with a detection limit of 0.2 pM within 15 min without washing steps (Fig. 3 ). The one-step NanoPISA used for the detection of SARS-CoV-2 NAs in clinical samples produced good results, which was similar to that obtained by the gold-standard seroneutralization test and the surrogate virus-neutralizing ELISA. This proves for the first time that other GNPs also have better SPR coupling enhancement properties than CG.

Gold nanostructures with anisotropic shapes(i.e. spheres,stars,cubes,rods,prisms)possess LSPR, which arises from collective oscillations of electrons in the conduction band upon irradiation. Plasmonic properties and electromagnetic field enhancements of gold nanostructures could be tuned by controlling the shape and size of nanostructures as well as the local chemical environments.

DNA-mediated metal nanostructures with diverse morphologies, such as nano mushrooms, snowmen, dumbbells, nano cuboids, and nanoflowers, are widely utilized in enhanced SPRS due to their inherent tips and nanogaps within them. Zhou et al. synthesized Au@Au nanoflowers with multi-tip spatial structures or more large but fewer petals featuring nanogaps [73]. By manipulating the “lying” conformation of polyA on the gold surface, Au@Au mNFs with muti-tip spatial structures was obtained, leading to an accelerated longitudinal growth rate of gold atoms after nucleation on the gold core surface. This unique shape exhibits a remarkable enhancement in the EM field at the sensing interface.

The fabrication of metallic nanoparticles has seen significant progress towards morphology control and preparation of particles with sharp edges or tips, such as nano bipyramids, nanotriangles or nanostars. The gold bipyramids (GBPs) are expected to be a promising alternative to obtain high detection sensitivity due to its sharper tips compared with spherical or rod-shaped gold nanoparticles (GNs). Wu et al. synthesized a new SPR sensing substrate based on the use of graphene oxide and GBPs, which were modified with staphylococcal protein A (SPA) [74]. As a result, the present sensing substrate exhibits a good response to rabbit IgG in the concentration range of 0.15–40 μgmL−1, which is more sensitive than that based on MPA and GO-SPA-GNs. Gold nanostars provide tunable plasmon bands while their sharp branches acting as “lightning rod” and enhancing the local electromagnetic fields greatly. Wu et al. combined gold nanostars with carboxyl-functionalized graphene oxide (cGO) sheets [75]. Testing results show that cGO could immobilize more antibodies due to the abundant carboxylic groups on its surface, leading to fairly large improvements in the analytical performance.

Gold nanorods (GNRs) are attractive optical sensors because their LSPR properties are susceptible to the dielectric constant of the surrounding medium, which is regulated by biological binding [76]. In immunoassays, the researchers covalently conjugated antibodies to GNRs, and the specific binding of the target induced the evident peak shift of LSPR spectrum [77]. The peak redshift is linearly proportional to the target concentration. This strategy has been successfully applied to the highly sensitive detection of cancer biomarkers [78] and hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg) [79]. By using GNRs to detect tumor necrosis factor alpha (TNF-α). Law et al. [80] proved that GNRs is a promising amplification tag in SPR sensing technology. In the study, nanoparticles and immune detection technology were integrated into the SPR system, and the plasma field expansion from gold thin film to GNRs led to a sharp increase in sensitivity. Antibody-functionalized sensing membrane and antibody-coupled GNRs can be used as plasma coupling partners to form a powerful and ultra-sensitive sandwich immunoassay for the detection of cancer related diseases. It is found that compared with the traditional SPR biosensor technology, bio-coupled GNRs marker can enhance the detection signal of TNF - α more than 40 times.

The absorption spectrum of Gold nanorods (GNRs) shows two peaks corresponding to its transverse and longitudinal absorption bands respectively. The longitudinal absorption band is more sensitive to the change of dielectric constant of the surrounding medium. Nengsih et al. [81] studied the effect of morphology of gold nanostructure on the plasma sensing monitoring of formaldehyde in water. Seed-mediated method prepared two kinds of gold nanostructures (spherical and rod-like). The results showed that higher plasma sensitivity can be obtained by optimizing the arrangement of GNRs on the surface to minimize the plasma coupling effect.

Wang et al. [82] studied the enhancement effect of GNRs on the sensitivity of optical fiber SPR sensors. It was proposed to model the sensing effects of optical fiber SPR sensors decorated with metal nanoparticles. The sensitizing effect of GNRs was studied and demonstrated by simulation and experiments. Based on the mathematical model, GNRs with a length-to-width ratio of 5:1 was used to verify the sensitivity enhancement effect of GNRs. The results showed that with the width-to-height ratio increase, the sensing sensitivity of the refractive index increased at first and then tended to stabilize or decrease. After parameter optimization, the width-to-height ratio of GNRs is 8, the ratio of length-to-width is 12.5, and the sensitivity of refractive index is 4465.53 nm/RIU. A new method to improve the sensitivity of optical fiber SPR sensors is proposed and demonstrated.

Nanocomposites based on gold materials are increasingly critical structures for creating highly sensitive SPR bioassays. These analyses use the advantages of every element in the nanostructures. For example, a core-shell Ag@GNPs combined the strong plasmon and refractive index of Ag nanoparticles (AgNPs) with high chemical stability and low toxicity of GNPs. By storing Ag@GNPs on an indium tin oxide (ITO) substrate and then conjugating with recognition elements, such as biotin, different concentrations of Sa can be quantified in the linear detection range from 1 pM to 10 nM [83]. Prakashan et al. [84] proposed a vitamin A sensor based on SPR by embedding Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles into a SiO2–TiO2–ZrO2 ternary matrix to form a complex signal marker. The average size of Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles was 30 nm. The sample was coated on optical fiber, and its specificity for vitamin A sensing was analyzed by transmission spectrum. It is found that the new vitamin A sensor has a linear response to the concentration of vitamin A in the range of 10–1000 μM.

In addition, combining the detection antibody (Ab2) and magnetite nanoparticles (MNPs) can significantly improve the sensitivity of GNRs-based LSPR analysis. The use of MNPs not only magnifies the LSPR shift of GNRs but also makes the detection more convenient because MNPs can separate and enrich analytes from complex solutions under the action of external magnets. The high refractive index and high molecular weight of Fe3O4 make them powerful enhancers for plasma response to biological binding events, thus significantly improving the sensitivity, reliability, dynamic range, and calibration linearity of trace small molecule LSPR determination. Chen et al. [85] also proposed a magnetic activation capture and digital counting (mAC + DC) method, which uses magnetic plasma nanoparticles (MPNPs) to accelerate single molecule sensing. The Spiky Fe3O4@GMPNPs with a fixed target-specific probe is "activated" by binding to the target microRNA (miRNA) and then transported through a volume fluid to the capture probe on the photonic crystal (PC) through magnetically driven transport. Through the spectral matching of the LSPR of MPNPs and the resonance guided by PC, each captured MPNP can locally quench the PC reflection so that the captured MPNPs can be seen separately, with high contrast and easy to count. In the study, the quantitative detection of miRNA-375 cancer biomarkers directly from unprocessed human serum was demonstrated, with a response time of 1 min, a detection limit of 61.9 aM, a wide dynamic range (100 aM–10 pM) and single base mismatch selectivity. This method is very suitable for the quantification of minimally invasive biomarkers, and short sample-response time makes it have potential application value in POCT. Oza et al. [86] synthesized seed-mediated gold-iron oxide core-shell nanoparticles and functionalized it with cy5-sulfhydryl single-stranded DNA probe to detect mutant DNA. The peak value of SPR with redshift determines that the optimum concentration of mercaptan DNA is 0.177 μM. It is possible to use the stable nanoparticles functionalized with this single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) probe to detect DNA mutation, and the lowest target DNA concentration detected by this method is up to 1.2 × 10- 11 mol/L.

Compared with the traditional SPR biosensors, some unique modification methods can significantly improve the sensitivity of the SPR biosensors, such as molybdenum sulfide modified with GNPs (GNPs@MoS2). The edge or defect position of the MoS2 nanoparticles is selectively adjusted with GNPs, which not only showed the physical and chemical properties of GNPs and MoS2 but also extended the additional functions of new catalytic, magnetic, and optoelectronic nanomaterials [51]. The introduction of graphene and WS2 also improved the sensing performance. The interaction between these nanomaterials with good properties, such as high light absorption, can produce many eye-catching phenomena [87]. For example, the monolayer heterogeneous nanosheet structures of graphene and WS2 are effective in detecting the interaction between BSA and anti-BSA antibodies in plasma-based detection systems. This detection technique does not require any complex functionalization scheme because the gold chip wrapped in graphene-WS2 nanoflakes can be attached to protein molecules through hydrophobic interaction. The LOD of the anti-BSA antibody is 0.44 μg/mL. Such as GNPs growth on COFs frameworks(GNPs/COFs). The photoreduction deposition method is used to generate larger and more uniformly distributed spherical GNPs on the surface of COFs(Fig. 4 ). Not only does it exhibit a significant SERS enhancement effect, but it also exhibits excellent stability and repeatability. The application of the new substrate in the detection of macrolide antibiotics successfully captured the characteristic peaks of four antibiotics (erythromycin ethylsuccinate, erythromycin ester, madecassoside and rapamycin). The limit of detection (LOD) was increased by about 1–3 orders compared with some previously reported techniques, such as electrochemical, fluorescent and colorimetric methods [88].

Liao et al. [89] proposed a SPR sensor based on two-dimensional (2D) materials with blue phosphorus (BP) and transition metals (TMDCs). In the sensor, BP/TMDCs is coated on ITO and different metals (Au/Ag/Cu) to improve sensitivity. By optimizing the structure parameters, the angular sensitivity can reach 226.0°/RIU using BP/WS2 monolayer and Au film. When 4-layer BP/MoS2, 228 nm ITO, and 25 nm Au thin films are used, the phase sensitivity is 3.6001 × 106 deg/RIU, which is 6.77 times higher than that of Au-ITO structure and 54.40 times higher than that of traditional Au thin films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gold nanoparticles enhance electrochemical detection signals
ce:section.ce:para: Gold nanoparticles (GNPs) have been increasingly used in biocatalysis and electrochemical biosensors due to their unique physicochemical properties. In recent years, many major diseases marked by cancer have had an increasingly significant impact on human health and life. Therefore, accurate detection of certain nucleic acids, proteins, and tumor markers is essential. Electrochemical sensors that combine various biological strategies are emerging one after another, and their performance is very outstanding. In electrochemical biological nucleic acid sensors, due to the small size of the detection target, the platform and medium where the sensor is located must also be specific to the environment, and the requirements for the platform are high, such as controlling the surface area of Au electrodes and glassy carbon electrodes. On the other hand, in order to improve the sensitivity and specificity of sensors, the application of nanomaterials in sensor manufacturing is also based on the continuous development of various physical materials.

Gold itself has conductivity, and nano gold also has good conductivity, which can enhance the conductivity of the original electrode. Due to their electrochemical properties, GNPs are more suitable for electrochemical biosensors [90]. In addition, the special nature of GNPs surface (with free electrons) will form surface plasmon resonance effect under the action of incident light, and a characteristic peak will appear in the ultraviolet visible range. After synthesizing gold nanoparticles, only UV characterization is needed to determine whether the preparation is successful. This method can also be used to verify whether DNA modification on GNPs is successful (if DNA modification is successful, its volume will slightly increase and move towards longer wavelengths compared to the peak position of GNPs themselves). Due to the plasmon coupling effect between gold nanoparticles, the dispersion and aggregation process of gold nanoparticles will be accompanied by a color change from red to blue, which can be observed by the naked eye. Many environmental changes such as salinity can affect the degree of aggregation and dispersion of gold nanoparticles, making it a universal example for developing colorimetric biosensors.

Nanomaterials have been widely used in electrochemical biosensing systems for highly sensitive and selective detection of various biological targets [91]. Gold nanoparticles themselves have unique advantages and can also be combined with other nanomaterials to synergistically amplify signals. Based on sea urchin shaped carbon nanotube gold nanoparticle (CNT-GNPs) nanoclusters [92], Han et al. developed a simple, label free, and ultra sensitive sandwich type electrochemical DNA biosensor for signal amplification in DNA detection (as shown in (Fig. 5 ) [93]. Specifically, the electrochemical polymerization of dopamine (DA) was used to modify gold electrodes, and amino modified probe DNA (NH2-ssDNA) was covalently attached to the surface of polydopamine (PDA) functionalized gold electrodes through Schiff base reaction to prepare DNA biosensors [94]. The catechol functional group of PDA was coupled with amino modified DNA to anchor the DNA probe at the interface of PDA. At this point, it is in an untargeted state, which is the original state. When a target (T-DNA) is present, double stranded DNA functionalized GNPs are introduced into the sensor by hybridizing with P-DNA, exposing a site. Afterwards, the end modified single-walled carbon nanotubes hybridize with DNA at specific sites and connect to the surface of gold nanoparticles, forming three-dimensional radial nanoclusters that exhibit significant electrochemical responses. Due to the flexibility of DNA chains, the relatively large mass of GNPs, and the large contact surface area of conjugated CNTs, CNT GNPs nanoclusters are located near the surface of the gold electrode, exhibiting highly efficient electron transfer [95]. So, using simple electrochemical characterization techniques such as cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), the feasibility of the preparation process and performance of DNA biosensors can be verified. Due to the larger contact surface area and superelectronic conductivity of carbon nanotube gold nanoparticles, this newly designed 3D DNA nanostructure exhibits ultra sensitive detection of DNA, with a detection limit of 5.2 fM (linear range of 0.1 pmol/L to 10 nmol/L), and high selectivity in distinguishing single mismatched DNA from perfectly matched target DNA in optimal environments. This biosensor combines the synergistic properties of carbon nanotubes and gold nanoparticles, demonstrating a highly accurate and specific signal amplification strategy for achieving sensitive electrochemical biosensors for DNA detection and diagnostic applications.

Gold nanoparticles (GNPs) have been developed and applied in electrochemical biosensors, as a single gold nanoparticle can almost load hundreds of DNA strands, providing significant amplification for target detection. Combined with other strategies, the target to be tested can be specifically detected with a relatively low detection limit. Ge et al. designed a clustered regular interval short palindromic repeat (CRISPR)/Cas12a mediated dual-mode electrochemical biosensor (Fig. 6 ) for detecting transgenic soybean SHZD32-1, which does not require polymerase chain reaction (PCR) amplification [96]. Synthesized functionalized composite bio nanomaterials Fe3O4@GNPs/Using DNA Fc&Ru as the signal unit, select the characteristic gene fragment of SHZD32-1 as the target DNA (tDNA) [97]. When Cas12a, crRNA, and tDNA coexist, a ternary complex Cas12a crRNA tDNA is formed, which activates the non-specific cleavage ability of the CRISPR/Cas12a system towards single stranded DNA. Therefore, the single stranded DNA Fc in the signal unit is cleaved, resulting in a decrease in the fast sweep voltammetry (FSV) signal of ferrocene (Fc) and an increase in the electrochemiluminescence (ECL) signal of ruthenium complex (Ru) inhibited by Fc. The addition of GNPs amplifies the signal several times and achieves the expected detection limit. Among them, the linear ranges of ECL and FSV are 1-107 fM/L and 10-108 fM/L, respectively, and the detection limits of ECL and FSV are 0.3 fM/L and 3 fM/L, respectively. The accuracy, precision, stability, selectivity, and reliability are all satisfactory. In addition, PCR free detection can be completed within 1 h at room temperature without the need for complex operations and sample processing, demonstrating great potential in the practical application detection of genetically modified crops. And it can help develop more environmentally friendly electrochemical biosensors based on GNPs. And due to the cross validation of electrochemiluminescence (ECL) and fast sweep voltammetry (FSV) signals, the accuracy and reliability are high. This biosensor provides a new solution for highly sensitive, accurate, and specific on-site detection of genetically modified organisms, and offers new ideas for the application of CRISPR/Cas12a systems in sensor development. For the manufacturing of electrochemical biosensors without biological amplification strategies, GNPs are an important component of the amplification signal.

Gold nanoparticles are widely used due to their excellent conductivity, as well as their small size and large specific surface area. When combined with other biological nanomaterials, they have better performance in electrochemical biosensors. The determination of T4 multinucleotide kinase (PNK) activity and screening of PNK inhibitors are crucial for disease diagnosis and drug discovery [98]. Many electrochemical strategies have been applied for sensitive measurement of PNK activity and inhibition, however, previous strategies often involved multiple steps in the detection process of other molecular markers and electrochemical readout. Here we present an electrochemical DNA (E-DNA) sensor for one-step detection of PNK, which has a "signal on" readout function and does not require additional molecular marker modification. In Fig. 7 , Lin et al. designed a highly switchable double stranded DNA (dsDNA) probe immobilized on gold nanoparticle modified molybdenum disulfide nanoparticles (MoS2-GNPs) [99], which have a large surface area and high conductivity for improving signal gain in PNK detection. This "Signal-ON" E-DNA sensor integrated with MoS2-GNPs has higher sensitivity than sensors without MoS2-GNPs, with a detection limit of 2.18 × 104 U/mL. In addition, the analysis results of the system show high selectivity, which can distinguish PNK from other enzymes and proteins, and can be used for screening inhibitors. MoS2-GNPs have been widely used as electrode modification materials to enhance the conductivity of ultra sensitive detection of small molecules, proteins, and nucleic acids. This sensor combines the advantages of electrochemical DNA sensors and MoS2-GNPs: (1) E-DNA sensors are easy to manufacture and can directly detect PNK in complex samples with high sensitivity, demonstrating the potential for clinical applications; (2) Compared with the reported "Signal-OFF" biosensor with maximum signal suppression limitation, the "Signal-ON" electrochemical signal generated by this sensor is more stable; (3) The convenience and signal stability of one-step and reagent free sensors enable real-time monitoring of PNK activity; (4) MoS2-GNPs not only provide an effective DNA fixation area, but also accelerate the electron transfer of electrochemically labeled molecules, enhance Faraday current, and thus improve the sensitivity of PNK detection. Experimental results have shown that this E-DNA sensor can sensitively detect PNK in buffer and cell lysate, screen for PNK inhibitors, and further be used for disease diagnosis and drug discovery.

Gold nanoparticles play a strong role in amplifying signals in electrochemical biosensors, and through their conductivity, electrochemical properties, and local surface plasmon resonance of GNPs, more accurate and sensitive sensors can be prepared. They have great potential for future applications in chemical analysis, biosensing, disease diagnosis, and other fields.

Surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) technology is a non-destructive molecular detection technique that reflects the vibrational and rotational energy information of molecular bonds. It is a "fingerprint" spectrum that can specifically identify molecules and is widely used in quantitative analysis, sensing detection, catalysis, biology, and energy research fields Under normal circumstances, the Raman signal intensity of ordinary molecules is very low, and it is necessary to enhance them with precious metal nanomaterials to achieve high-sensitivity molecular detection, known as "surface enhancement technology" Surface enhancement technology typically uses precious metal materials such as gold, silver, and copper. The nanoparticles of these materials generate collective electron oscillations on the surface under excitation light, known as surface plasmon resonance (SPR) modes [101]. This mode can break through the diffraction limit of light and convert the excitation light into a localized electromagnetic field ("hotspot") confined to several nanometer spaces on the surface of the precious metal material. This local electromagnetic field has extremely high intensity and can concentrate the incident light energy in a narrow nanoscale space, thereby amplifying the Raman spectral signal of nearby probe molecules, known as electromagnetic field enhanced Raman scattering. At the same time, the excited hot electrons in precious metal materials undergo charge transfer with the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lower unoccupied molecular orbital (LOMO) of the molecule, resulting in an increase in molecular polarizability and the formation of chemically enhanced Raman scattering [102]. Due to the fact that these two enhancement methods often coexist and have an effect, they are collectively referred to as SERS technology.

Because most inorganic nanoparticles have unique LSPR properties, they are widely used in Raman spectroscopy to improve the sensitivity of Raman signals of probe molecules, often referred to SERS [17,103]. SERS can be used as a molecular detection probe for pico-level detection [104]. With the development of nanotechnology, surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy has made great progress, and its application has been extended to many fields, such as chemical sensing, material science, biomedical research, art, forensic medicine, and so on. Raman spectra provide unique chemical and structural information for specific species, but in most cases, the sensitivity of conventional Raman detection is insufficient because of the relatively low cross-section of inelastic scattering Raman photons. The size of the Raman cross-section can be significantly enhanced when the Raman active molecules are placed on or near the rough precious metal surface. Various plasma nanoparticles or rough metal substrates have been found to enhance SERS signals with an enhancement factor as high as 1014. Among the multiple applications of SERS spectroscopy, the biochemical detection of IVDs has attracted wide attention because of its high sensitivity and fingerprint recognition ability. This enhanced performance is obtained using silver or gold colloid as a SERS substrate. In particular, compared with cadmium-containing quantum dots (QDs) and other toxic or immunogenic nanoparticles, GNPs is considered a good candidate for biomedical applications because of their long-term stability, good biocompatibility, easy-to-control size distribution, and high uniformity. In addition, GNPs can effectively scatter visible light without flickering or photobleaching, which can amplify the Raman scattering efficiency of adsorbed species and achieve spectral determination of species at the single molecular level, even under environmental conditions [105] (Fig. 8 ).

The immunoassay of antibody-coupled GNPs based on SERS has attracted wide attention because of its rapid and sensitive detection ability. Scaglione, F. et al. [118] prepared a biosensor for the selective detection of human serum albumin (HSA) based on the unique plasma properties of nanoporous gold (NPG). Anti-HSA antibody was covalently grafted onto gold ligament by 4-ATP raman probe, and NPG was immunized to obtain nano-hybrid NPG-4-ATP-Ab with SERS activity to obtain the particular binding characteristics needed for the detection of molecules in biological diagnosis. The sensor can detect HSA at ultra-low concentration, and the detection range is 0.1 ng/L - 0.1 mg/L. To improve the LOD and reduce cross-reactivity of Zika virus (ZIKV) and dengue virus (DENV), Camacho, S. A. et al. [119] synthesized gold shell isolated nanoparticles (Au-SHINs) by coating Nile blue (raman reporter molecule) with 100 nm GNPs and 4 nm thick silica shell. Then, the SERS nanoprobe is wrapped in the final silica shell, and the specificity required for immunoassay applications is provided by modifying the outer surface of the nanoprobe with ZIKV antibodies. The SERS nanoprobe reported here can detect a shallow concentration, and there is no cross-reaction with DENV nonstructural protein 1 (NS1) antigen when the concentration of ZIKV NS1 is reduced to 10 ng/mL. Carneiro, M. C. C. G. et al. [120] reported an innovative combination of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP) and natural antibodies in SERS to accurately detect CEA. The MIP material serves as a preconcentration scheme for the target protein, while the natural antibody is responsible for signaling the presence of CEA on the MIP platform. For sensitive SERS detection, MIP membranes were first incubated in samples containing CEA and then set in SERS markers. For SERS labeling, GNSs was used as a metal carrier, 4-ATP as a raman reporter molecule, and CEA natural antibody as a recognition element. The sensitivity of the whole system is as low as 1.0 ng/mL. This innovative combination solves the problems caused by using only MIP materials or natural antibody sandwich methods for SERS detection. The results confirm the accuracy of CEA detection and the possibility of generating quantitative information and are considered an excellent alternative to the current method.

The combination of GNPs based on aptamer functionalization has also been applied to SERS biosensors, connecting nucleic acid aptamers with specificity and high affinity to the surface of GNPs. Compared with traditional recognition elements such as antibodies, it has the advantages of stable environmental temperature, a slight difference between different batches, easy synthesis, small size, easy chemical modification, and lack of immunogenicity. The recognition of biological species, such as disease biomarkers, can be readily achieved via an on/off approach. For example, recently, Ma et al. [121] detected exosomal miRNA-21 from human blood using a stable rhodamine 6G (R6G) reporter in the duplex-specific nuclease (DSN) assisted SERS experiment. The R6G was encapsulated in Au/Ag compound NPs, while the capturing probe was designed by modifying silica microbeads with the miRNA-21 recognition aptamer. The SERS probe was conjugated with the capturing probe via Au–S, and upon exposure to the target, the SERS signal was enhanced by introducing DSN assisted DNA cleaving. Pu et al. [114] used Cy3 labeled aptamers and gold-silver shell-core nanoparticles (Au@AgCSNPs) marked complementary chains constructing SERS sensors with a detection range of 1.0 × 10−13 - 1.0 × 10−9 M, the detection limit reaches 2.75 fM. This method has high sensitivity and good selectivity and can be applied to the detection of E2 in water samples.

One of the commonly adopted detection approaches from designing the aptamer-SERS biosensors is to form a target “sandwich” between capturing platform and output signal unit, so that the target can be determined through raman signals from the label molecules. Considering the use of aptamers, the hybridization of complementary DNA strands and the interaction of streptavidin-biotin may be the most adopted protocols in the sandwich-type assays. A general sandwich protocol well recommended in designing the aptamer-SERS biosensors is to make aptamers anchored on a solid substrate, which can thus capture the target molecules via aptamer-target interaction. Then, a secondary target recognition element is introduced, which can bind the substrate to produce SERS signals. For example, Kukushkin's group [122] demonstrated thesandwich formation by capturing the influenza virus (IV) between the AgNPs substrate and the secondary aptamers.

Using the magnetic and optical properties of magnetic beads and SERS labeling based on biomolecule functionalized GNP, this SERS immunoassay combined with magnetic beads does not need a complex separation procedure, and the detection speed is breakneck. Chen, Q. S. et al. [116] used the amino-terminal AFB1 aptamer (NH2-DNA1) and mercaptan terminal AFB1 complementary aptamer (SH-DNA2) conjugated with magnetic beads (CS–Fe3O4) as enrichment probes, GNRs@DNTB@AgNRs (ADANRs) were used as reporting nanoprobes to develop a SERS adapter sensor for the ultra-sensitive detection of AFB1. 5,5-dithiobis (2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DNTB) is embedded in gold-silver core-shell nanorods as a raman reporter molecule, which has a large raman scattering cross section and no fluorescence interference. CS-Fe3O4 has good biocompatibility and superparamagnetism, which can be used for rapid signal concentration. Therefore, NH2-DNA1-CS-Fe3O4 and SH-DNA2-ADANRs were prepared by the hybridization reaction of aptamers and complementary aptamers. When AFB1 exists, AFB1 will competitively combine with NH2-DNA1-CS-Fe3O4, causing SH-DNA2-ADANRs to dissociate from CS-Fe3O4, thus further reducing SERS signal (Fig. 9 ). Based on the sensor, the detection limit of AFB1 is 0.0036 ng/mL, the linear range is 0.01–100 ng/mL, and the correlation coefficient is 0.986.

In particular, MNPs based sandwich approach is quite helpful in biomedical POCT. Zhou, T. et al. [117] successfully prepared a highly sensitive sandwich nanosensor based on Fe3O4 and Au@ATP@Ag nanorods to detect histamine. Fe3O4 beads containing ethyl acetate are used as trapping parts to enrich histamine. Then Au@ATP@Ag core-shell nanorods functionalized by Nα, Nα-bis (carboxymethyl)-l-lysine (AB-NTA) were used to connect with the imidazolyl group of histamine, and the internal standard 4-ATP in the core-shell structure was used as SERS signal. The least squares regression model shows a linear relationship in the concentration range of 10−3 - 10−8 mol/L, R2 = 0.9907. This new method can quickly and reliably detect histamine in fish samples and RAW264.7 cell lysates. This histamine extraction and SERS analysis method makes it possible to develop ultra-sensitive histamine detection methods. Hu, C. M. et al. [123] combined SERS immunoassay with magnetic separation to detect cTnI ultra sensitively and rapidly. The SERS tags based on gold-silver core-shell bimetallic nanoparticles were synthesized using 4-MBA as raman reporter molecule, and then they were combined with antibodies to form a SERS immunoprobe. In the presence of cTnI, SERS tags and capture probes form sandwich structures through immune reaction, and then immune complexes were enriched through the specific response of streptavidin and biotin on the surface of magnetic beads. Finally, the concentration of biomarkers was quantified by detecting the intensity of the characteristic raman peaks of report molecules. On this SERS immune platform, the LOD of cTnI is 9.80 pg/mL, and cTnI has no cross-reaction with non-specific proteins, so it has good specificity.

In order to maximize the enhancement factor (EF) of SERS signals, it is necessary to obtain reporter molecules with more vital electromagnetic properties. The key to obtain a solid electric field region is to accurately control the shape, size, and spatial structure of plasma nanostructures (density, uniformity, nanoparticle gap, etc.). The control of these parameters benefits from many lithography technologies, such as electron beam lithography, optical lithography, nanospheres lithography, and nano-imprint lithography. Several teams have verified the designs, such as plasma nanodisks, nanodimers, and nanorods, and the EF can reach 106 - 109. Multimodal gold nanoparticles refer to nanoparticles modified and hybridized with two or more methods, which can endow the particles with more diverse biological functions [124].

For example, Barbillon et al. [125] prepared eight vertically oriented 1 cm2 self-assembled ZnO nanocolumn arrays by pulsed laser deposition, and then 30 nm gold was coated by thermal evaporation or electron beam evaporation. Each sample had a different set of ZnO and Au growth conditions. Then, Au/ZnO nanoarrays were tested for SERS detection of thiophenol molecules. Using the ratio of (SERS)/(Raman) as the index of SERS sensitivity, it was found that the ratio varied from 1.7 to 23.7 in 8 samples. Based on fixing all the growth parameters of each pair, the effects of density, width, filling factor, orientation, uniformity, and shape of nanostructures on the average SERS strength and the in-wafer reproducibility of SERS response were considered, and 9 pairs were compared. In general, the smaller width of nanorods is related to the stronger average SERS signal, while the vertical array with a higher filling factor shows better in-wafer reproducibility.

Matricardi et al. [108] proposed a scalable method for the design of plasma supercrystal arrays based on template-assisted gold nanospheres and topologically patterned polydimethylsiloxane moulds. A regular square supercrystal collection with the hexagonal arrangement was realized, with a period of up to 400 nm, a feature size of about 200 nm, and an area of 0.5 cm2. These 2D supercrystals showed well-defined collective equal ion exciton modes, which could be tuned from visible to near-infrared by simply changing the lattice parameters. They simulated the physical origin of the mixed mode and demonstrate the application of superlattice arrays as SERS spectral substrates, which could be tailored for specific detection lasers. Therefore, we studied the effects of lattice parameters, local order degree, and cluster structure to determine the best configuration of efficient SERS for Raman probe under 785 nm excitation.

The gain of 1–2 orders of magnitude of EF (106 - 109) can also be achieved by inserting a metal layer into the plasma nanosystem, such as nanodiscs, nanoholes et al. This gain is realized by the hybridization between the surface plasmon or local plasmon mode on the metal film and the imaging mode in the plasma substrate. Among those designs, nanodimers, like a bowtie (two triangles facing each other), and disc dimers, separated by nano-gaps, are the most preferred since they provide a strong field enhancement in the gap [126–128]. Due to the significant localization of electromagnetic fields caused by the coupling between the adjacent nanoparticles, the SERS enhancement increases significantly with the decrease of gap size in nanodimers.

In the SERS analysis based on metal nanoparticles, the Raman scattering intensity of molecules at "hot spots" is usually located at the junctions and voids of interacting metal nanostructures (such as dimers or aggregates), and the Raman scattering intensity may be several orders of magnitude higher than that on the surface of individual nanoparticles. Therefore, GNPs aggregates are very popular nanostructures in SERS, and their applications range from single-molecule Raman spectroscopy to supersensitive imaging of living cells. Ghosh et al. [129] reported a directional microwave-assisted self-assembly of gold nanodimers, which is a strategy involving self-assembly that exploits selective heterogenous nucleation to create graphene separated Au dimers. Furthermore, the dimers are made by selective heterogenous nucleation of a second layer of Au particles on graphene-covered Au particles at a specific temperature window by controlling the kinetics of the reduction reaction. The SERS substrate of 7 × 106 cm−2 Au-Graphene-Au dimer was obtained by uniform separation of graphene monolayer on 1 cm2 substrate [130]. The Raman spectrum signal of graphene is increased 35 times from a single dimer, and the intensity EF of each molecule is 107. The detection sensitivity of rhodamine 6G can reach ppb level. Lim et al. [131] prepared GNPs heterodimers by controlling the number of linked DNA molecules on the surface of GNPs through magnetic separation. These DNA-bound GNPs heterodimers were allowed to clamp only one Raman-active Cy3 dye molecule in the gap of the dimer. By controlling the thickness of the Ag shell grown on the surface of dimer GNPs, the Au/Ag core-shell nano-bell structure with an adjustable gap was obtained. Zhang et al. [132] developed a SERS based on a single base extension reaction for DNA methylation analysis. The capture probe modified by GNPs reacted with cyanine 5-deoxyribonucleoside triphosphate (Cy5-dGTP). A strong SERS signal was generated by adding GNPs to enhance the local electromagnetic field in the GNPs aggregate. Cui et al. [106] developed a simple SERS sensing strategy with a three-dimensional (3D) DNA nano-machine for ATP detection, which cleaved through exonuclease III (Exo III), leading to the formation of GNPs aggregates, resulting in the enhancement of electromagnetic fields. Depending on the target-activated Exo III cleavage, the 3D nano-machine can walk along the three-dimensional trajectory of the GNPs surface and generate self-assembly hotspots to enhance the SERS signal of Raman dyes, thus achieving a uniform determination of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) concentration with high sensitivity and repeatability. Under the optimized experimental conditions, the dynamic range of the biosensor for detecting adenosine triphosphate was expanded from 1 pM to 1 × 105 pM, and the detection limit was up to 0.29 pM.

Small nanoparticle aggregates can be prepared by introducing aggregation inducers, such as dyes, peptides, salts, or targets to be detected. Manikas et al. [107] reported that single GNPs to submicron GNPs aggregates can be produced by adjusting the ratio of polypeptide molecules to the number of GNPs. The aggregated particles were used for the SERS detection of adenine.

Based on the microstructure of polycrystalline ice, Bekana et al. [133] developed a new method to assemble large-area GNPs superstructures as SERS substrates without needing external templates and aggregators. silicon wafer (1 cm × 1 cm) was cleaned with ethanol repeatedly followed by water.Then 20 μL of GNP colloidal solution was dropped on the chip of silicon wafer and placed in the refrigerator at −20 °C for 20 min.Then the sample was kept at room temperature until it was completely dried and ready for use as a SERS substrate. After loading 10 μL of diﬀerent concentrations of CV ethanolic solution on the GNP superstructures and then leaving them at room temperature for 5min, the SERS spectra were collected. (Fig. 10 ). The GNPs superstructure has high SERS activity, and the EF is about 7.63 × 107, which is due to the existence of high-density hot spots in the whole superstructure. The SERS activity increased with particle size and aggregate size of the GNPs superstructure. In addition, the substrate had good consistency and reproducibility, and the relative standard deviations are 11.9 % and 12.4 % respectively. The substrate showed long-term stability and maintained SERS activity for five months, while the morphology of the superstructure did not change significantly. The substrate had been further applied to the unlabeled detection of trace thiram in apple fruits with a sensitivity as high as 0.28 ng/cm2, which provided great potential for monitoring the level of thiram in food and environmental samples.

Semiconductor materials have a large specific surface area, high electron mobility, thermal conductivity, excellent structural controllability, and more stable physical and chemical properties. These extraordinary properties make it a critical component for different applications in biosensors and imaging. These hybrid nanostructures based on semiconductors (silicon, graphene, etc.) and GNPs have high biocompatibility and low production costs. In addition, they allow hot spots to appear at the interface level between metals and semiconductors, which can significantly enhance SERS signals. Magno et al. [134] studied the Au/Si SERS sensor optimization for supersensitive chemical detection. Au/Si nanosensor used a low-cost and rapid fabrication method composed of anisotropic reactive ion etching and electron beam evaporation of gold layer. In this study, the thickness of the gold layer varied from 10 to 50 nm, and thiophenol molecules with concentrations between 10−12 and 10−3 M were selected as model molecules. The optimization method was to study the effect of the gold layer thickness on the detection limit of thiophenol molecules on fixed-shaped silicon nanorods. It was found that the LOD of thiophenol using Au/Si nanocolumn is between 9 × 10−12 and 3 × 10−8 M. In addition, the thickest gold layer could reach the best detection limit. Finally, the numerical analysis based on 3D finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) confirmed the experimental results. Wang et al. [110] used 2D graphene oxide (GO) loaded GNPs to prepare Au nanorod arrays (GNRAs). GNPs/GO hybrid materials could avoid the aggregation of GNPs, thus providing better stability for SERS devices. Different proportions of GO and GNPs were prepared, the coupling properties of GNRAs-GO/GNPs were studied, and the GNRAs-GO/GNPs substrate supported on 1,2-bis (4-pyridine) ethylene (BPE) was optimized. The addition of GO/GNPs gave GNRAs generally high adsorption of analytes, and the electromagnetic mechanism of multi-dimensional coupling of GNRAs-GO/GNPs enhanced the Raman properties, so the sensitivity and repeatability of GNRAs-GO/GNPs preparation were obtained. The LOD of melamine in liquid milk is 1 ppm, which aligned with the maximum limit of melamine in infant milk powder proposed by the World Health Organization (WHO).

The composite composed of GNPs and inorganic elements provides a simple and general design strategy for constructing SERS biosensors and has great application potential in food safety monitoring and clinical diagnosis. Runowski et al. [135] successfully prepared core/shell luminescent plasma materials (Nd3+:YAS@Au), which was a composite of rare earth doped microspheres (about 50 μm) and GNPs (about 10–20 nm). This material combined the luminescence properties and plasma activity of Nd3+-doped microspheres in a single-micron material. The luminescent plasma microspheres are used as active SERS substrates to detect organic probes. Due to the existence of GNPs, the Raman signals of organic compounds (probes) adsorbed on the surface of microspheres are enhanced 10 times, and the bifunctional luminescence-plasma materials can be used to detect many kinds of organic compounds at the same time. Xie et al. [136] developed a new method to prepare hollow and porous nitrogen-doped carbon (NC) nanoballoons (NBs) supported GNPs (GNC-NBs) with large specific surface area and high N and Au content. Compared with the previously reported sensors, the SERS adaptation sensor based on the prepared GNC-NBs had a wider linear range (10 - 107 cells/mL), lower LOD (3 cells/mL), and better selectivity for bacterial detection. Notably, the GNC-NBs SERS aptamer sensor also effectively detected bacteria in real food and biological samples.

By integrating plasma-active GNPs into the structure of supporting polymer materials, an exciting sensing method is proposed, which meets the biocompatibility requirements and has high stability and detection sensitivity. Wu et al. [112] demonstrated a one-step process of polymer-induced interfacial self-assembly to prepare flexible, robust, reproducible, and independent SERS substrates (GNPs/Polyvinyl chloride (PVC) films). In this method, polymer (PVC) played a dual role: to induce the self-assembly of nanoparticles at the interface and fix the assembled nanostructures in the PVC matrix. Because the assembled nanoparticles were orderly and semi-embedded in the GNPs/PVC film, the PVC film had excellent repeatability and stability. In addition, the thin films could be easily regenerated by rinsing with NaBH4 solution. As a proof of concept, the film was directly coated on the apple surface for in-situ detection of pesticide residues, and the lower LOD of 10 ng/cm2 thiram was obtained. Moreover, with the help of a portable Raman spectrometer, the rapid detection of various pesticide residues in the field has been proven feasible. Because of its simple preparation process, good reliability, stability, and reusability, GNPs/PVC thin films have great potential in practical applications. Lehman et al. [137] prepared a complex composite containing GNPs that prepared highly hydrophilic poly (hydroxyethyl methacrylate) (pHEMA) matrix structure by 3D printing, in which GNPs were embedded by in-situ reduction. This composite has dual functions, which is not only a host scaffold for cell lines such as osteoblast, but also a plasma biosensor for in situ measurement of living cells. After that, the physicochemical and spectral properties of the composites, as well as the sensing ability of bioanalysis in vitro, were studied. The preosteoblast cell line was used to demonstrate its effectiveness as a biocompliant scaffold for cell growth and development, and its sensing properties were used to measure in situ the characteristics of cell metabolism in living cells in a discrete and non-invasive manner.

Compared with plasma nanosystems composed of individual metals, bimetallic nanosystems may provide better plasma resonance and chemical properties. Because silver has better plasma enhancement than gold, gold-silver bimetallic nanosystems have been developed to inhibit the oxidation of gold and silver. Therefore, due to the hot spots caused by LSPR coupling between Au and Ag nanosystems, the sharper and stronger characteristics of LSPR in bimetallic systems make it possible to obtain more significant SERS activity. Firstly et al. [36] assembled two types of SERS active substrates by preparing two kinds of metal nanoparticles. The GNF-Ag nanoparticles (GNPs) nuclear-satellite module driven by aptamers showed an amplified SERS signal, and the microcystins LR in Taihu Lake was detected sensitively. The LOD was 8.6 ± 0.4 pM. Feng et al. [37] established aptamers-driven GNRs-GNPs core-satellite assemblies as a SERS sensor for detecting mucin-1, a specific breast cancer marker protein. The LOD was 4.3 aM, and the linear range was 0.005–1 fM.

Su et al. [38] reached an improved sensitivity in the detection of cardiorenal syndrome markers by using the combination of 3D-ordered macroporous Au–Ag–Au array (substrate) and Ag–Au nanostars (nanotags). Thus, this combination generated hotspots from the plasmonic coupling in the near-field, inducing a SERS enhancement. The LODs observed experimentally with this plasmonic nanosystem (substrate + nanotags) were 0.41, 0.53, and 0.76 fg/mL for neutrophil gelatinase-associated lipocalin (NGAL), N-terminal prohormone of brain natriuretic peptide (NT-ProBNP), and cTnI respectively. Ning et al. [39] also studied the SERS properties of Au–Ag–Ag nanorods coupled to magnetic beads by DNA blotting. It was found that the LOD of human papillomavirus DNA 16 (HPV-16) fragment was 1 fM.

The structural modification of ordinary gold-silver bimetallic nanostructures can further improve the SERS signal strength and detection sensitivity. Zhang et al. [40] prepared core-shell plasma nanorods (PNRs) with adjustable closed nanogap based on coordination interaction and galvanic substitution. 4-mercaptopyridine (4-MP) was grafted on the core surface of GNRs to promote the controlled growth of the peripheral silver shell by forming a pyridine-Ag + coordination complex. Using HAuCl4 couple instead of Ag shell, the core-shell structure of Au–Ag with a uniform interstitial gap was obtained. The resulting PNRs can repeatedly amplify the Raman signal strength, providing a stable and quantitative SERS platform. PNRs was functionalized by circulating tumor cell-specific aptamers such as MCF-7 to test its applicability. Using SERS, the system can selectively detect as many as 20 cells of MCF-7 in simulated blood. The DNA dimer connected to the PNRs can also embed hydrophobic drug molecules such as adriamycin, thus increasing the versatility of the sensing platform, including drug delivery. Zhang et al. [41] reported a unique intelligent SERS nanoprobe, gold-4-mercapto-benzonitrile-Ag core-shell nanocomposites (Au@4-MBN@Ag) for the detection of endogenous H2S in living cells based on the reaction between silver shell and sulfide species. 4-MBN was selected as the SERS reporter to avoid the interference of cells and molecules. With the increase of sulfide concentration, silver sulfide (Ag2S) was continuously formed, which led to a decrease of the signal intensity in SERS. Au@4-MBN@Ag decreased gradually due to the weak SERS activity of Ag2S. By using nanoprobe, this method provided high sensitivity for H2S detection at the nanometer level and achieved the goal of background-free analysis. It had good anti-interference ability and has an excellent linear relationship with the sulfide concentration from 50 nM to 500 mM. The estimated LOD was 0.14 nM. This Au@4-MBN@Ag nanoprobe was successfully used to detect endogenous H2S in living Hep G2 cells stimulated by pyridoxal 5-phosphate monohydrate. This study provided a potential analysis method for the study of the physiological function of H2S. Yilmaz et al. [138] demonstrated a SERS activity with bimetallic core-shell nanoparticles with an intermediate layer of bioinspired polydopamine between Au and Ag nanoparticles. An EF value of 3.5 × 105 for the detection of MB molecules was found. This bioinspired polydopamine layer was employed as a stabilizing agent for the adsorption of silver nanoparticles and a reducing agent for the reduction of Ag ions.

Besides, Yin et al. [43] also prepared the bimetallic array composed of gold microring modified platinum disk by template self-assembly (TSA) process. As a result, the SERS of the 4-aminothiophenol molecule (4-ATP) was enhanced by 4.2 × 105 times. The detection limit of adenosine 4-triphosphate obtained from these superstructures is equivalent to the concentration of 1 nM.

Fluorescence resonance energy transfer is when the fluorescence spectrum of a fluorescent molecule (also known as a donor molecule) overlaps with the excitation spectrum of another fluorescent molecule (also known as an acceptor molecule), the excitation energy of the donor fluorescent molecule induces the acceptor molecule to emit fluorescence, while the fluorescence intensity of the donor fluorescent molecule itself decays. Simply put, it is the process of transferring energy from the donor to the acceptor mediated by a pair of dipoles in the excited state of the donor group. This process does not involve photons, so it is non radiative. After the donor molecule is excited, when the acceptor molecule is at a certain distance from the donor molecule and the energy difference between the vibrational energy levels of the ground state and the first electron excited state of the donor and acceptor is adapted to each other, the excited donor will transfer some or all of the energy to the acceptor, causing the acceptor to be excited. In the entire energy transfer process, there is no emission or reabsorption of photons involved. If the fluorescence quantum yield of the receptor is zero, energy transfer occurs and fluorescence is extinguished; If the receptor is also a fluorescent emitter, it exhibits the fluorescence of the receptor and causes a red shift in the secondary fluorescence spectrum. In addition, the metal enhanced fluorescence effect refers to the phenomenon where the fluorescence emission intensity of fluorescent molecules increases significantly relative to the fluorescence emission intensity of the free state when they approach the metal surface. The technology based on metal enhanced fluorescence principle has shown great potential for development in various fields such as chemical and biological sensing, biological imaging, single-molecule detection, DNA non-destructive testing, fluorescence resonance energy transfer immunoassay, etc. At present, research on metal enhanced fluorescence effects mainly focuses on solid thin film systems and has achieved good enhancement effects.

Plasma resonance energy transfer (PRET) occurs between plasma nanoparticles (NPs) and organic dyes, and due to its high sensitivity at the individual particle level, it has shown great potential in sensing chemistry. PRET is used for biosensing with extremely high sensitivity. PRET is an energy transfer process similar to FRET, which can occur between a plasma donor and a resonant acceptor, leading to corresponding changes in the Rayleigh scattering signal. The transfer efficiency largely depends on the degree of overlap between the scattering spectra of plasma nanoparticles and the absorption spectra of receptor molecules. Various nanoparticles have been prepared, including gold nanorods, gold nanospheres, silver nanospheres, and bimetallic nanoparticles, and used as plasma donors. Among these nanoprobes, gold nanoparticles (GNP) are highly favored not only for their high stability and good water solubility, but also for their visible scattering spectral bands and sensitive optical response to external stimuli.

The fluorescent biosensor is a recognized and dominant optical sensor. As one of the standard methods of disease detection in the field, it has the advantages of low cost, high sensitivity, ease of operation, and can even provide the detection sensitivity of a single molecule. It plays a vital role in biomedical diagnosis and biotechnology. Fluorescent molecules, including fluorescent dyes and fluorescent nanomaterials, are an essential part of constructing fluorescence sensors. Fluorescence probes based on traditional organic fluorescent dyes such as fluorescein and rhodamine are inefficient for biomolecule labeling. Low fluorescence intensity, high background noise, prone to "photobleaching" phenomenon, that is, the fluorescence weakens rapidly or even disappears under the irradiation of excited light. In recent years, fluorescence analysis based on nanomaterials can overcome the shortcomings of traditional fluorescent markers and has a great application prospect. These nanomaterials include QDs, silica nanoparticles doped with fluorescent dyes, Nanoparticles doped with upconversion lanthanide elements, carbon nanoparticles, precious metal nanoparticles, and so on [139–142]. Among these fluorescent nanosensors, fluorescence analysis based on GNPs has been widely concerned and studied because of its excellent light stability, biocompatibility, easy surface modification, and color persistence.

Fluorescence biosensors are usually designed using molecular beacons (MBs) based on the FRET effect, which is composed of fluorescent (donor) and quenching group (receptor) pairs. Generally speaking, if there is an inevitable overlap between the absorption spectrum of the receptor and the emission spectrum of the donor, fluorescence energy transferring from the donor to the recipient can be observed when the distance between the two fluorescent groups is less than 10 nm. Liu et al. [143] developed a switching fluorescent biosensor detecting of nucleic acid biomarkers. Due to FRET and photoinduced electron transfer, the current state was fluorescence "off". When the fluorescent probe specifically bound to the target, the rigid double-stranded DNA structure would be released from the surface of zirconium porphyrin metal-organic framework nanoparticles, resulting in the fluorescence "on" state. Observing the fluorescence on-off state allows the ultra-fast and ultra-sensitive detection of nucleic acid biomarkers realized within 30 min. In this section, we will introduce two kinds of fluorescence analysis based on GNPs. One is to use Au nanoclusters (GNCs) consisting of 2–100 Au atoms as fluorescence donors, and the other is to provide activable fluorescence analysis by using distance-dependent surface energy transfer generated by GNPs bound to the fluorescent donor (Fig. 11 ).

Compared with traditional organic quenchers, GNPs have unique structural and optical properties, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility, so GNPs have opened up the prospect of susceptible detection of bioactive molecules (including organic small molecules, macromolecular proteins, DNA, and pathogens) as excellent quenching agent.

To construct biosensors based on gold nano-fluorescence quenching materials, various fluorescent donor molecules, such as organic dyes, carbon nanodots, QDs, metal nanoclusters, and water-soluble fluorescent polymers, are firstly immobilized on the surface of GNPs by electrostatic interaction or covalent binding, resulting in fluorescence quenching. Then, the target molecule can selectively bind to the fluorescent probe and cause the fluorophore to be separated from the surface of GNPs. The fluorescence intensity recovered is proportional to the concentration of the target molecule to be measured [156].

Biological mercaptan exists mainly in the form of low molecular weight mercaptan or protein mercaptan, including cysteine (Cys), glutathione (GSH), homocysteine (Hcy), and protein cysteine residues. When the intracellular or external environment changes, it can be transformed into different oxidation intermediates, which plays a vital role in various physiological processes, such as maintaining redox balance, protein synthesis, and detoxification. Zhang et al. [157] designed a novel l-cysteine (L-Cys) fluorescence "off-on" probe based on the FRET between negatively charged amino-terminated porous silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) and positively charged citrate stabilized GNPs. The addition of L-Cys led to the release of adsorbed GNPs from the surface of SiNPs, thus restoring the fluorescence emission of SiNPs-ethanol (turn-on), which meant that the coordination ability of mercaptan with GNPs is more robust than that of electrostatic interaction. The fluorescence intensity of SiNPs-GNPs in ethanol is sensitive to L-Cys, and the restored fluorescence is proportional to the concentration of L-Cys. This method will be widely beneficial to developing new mercaptan biosensors based on nanostructured porous materials, and the proposed program is also expected to create a variety of functional nanoparticles to form other new nanosensors. Dong et al. [158] designed a novel infrared FRET fluorescence probe for L-Cys ratio detection. The system consisted of positively charged carbon dots (CDs) and negatively charged N-acetyl-l-cysteine-terminated GNPs (NAC-GNPs) to build FRET components. In the presence of NAC-GNPs, the fluorescence of CDs could be effectively quenched at 539 nm, while the emission of NAC-GNPs occurred at 630 nm. Subsequently, the interaction between L-Cys and NAC-GNPs led to a decrease in the emission intensity of NAC-GNPs. Still, due to the continuity of FRET efficiency, the emission intensity of CDs remained almost unchanged. The emission intensity ratio (I539/I630) at 539 nm–630 nm is linearly correlated with the concentration of L-Cys, and the LOD is 0.16 μM in the range of 1.0–110 μM. In addition, this far-red ratio sensor also showed excellent selectivity for L-Cys over other amino acids. It showed a very high potential in the diagnosis of cysteine-related diseases. GSH played a crucial role in biological systems and serves many cellular functions. As biological mercaptan contains mercaptan, carboxyl and amino groups, the differential detection of GSH, Cys, and Hcy is still challenging. Cong et al.
