[159] constructed a simple and environmentally friendly strategy, using Ce-based metal-organic skeleton and GNPs to detect GSH. The fluorescence intensity of Ce-MOFs was quenched by GNPs. The addition of GSH induced Ce-MOF/GNPs, which prevented the occurrence of FRET and the electrostatic interaction between Ce-MOFs and GNPs, thus further restoring the fluorescence of Ce-MOF. Under the optimized conditions, the "on" sensing process showed high selectivity to GSH and exemplary linearity in the 0.2–32.5 μM range, and the detection limit is as low as 58 nM. In addition, the practicability of the strategy is proved by analyzing the GSH in real human serum samples.

In recent years, biosensing in the NIR region has gotten great attention [160] due to its reduced photodamage, low background interference, and weak scattered light [161,162]. Developing preferable FRET systems in NIR region still remains a big challenge. Although GNPs are usually considered the star acceptors in FRET system, ordinary spherical GNPs typically do not absorb the NIR region light. Thus, different shapes of Au nanomaterials with varying absorption spectra have also been studied, such as nanorods, nanocubes, nano bipyramids, nanoplate, and various branched nanoparticles. Compared with single metal NCs such as GNCs or AgNCs, bimetallic NCs such as Au–Ag NCs have more attractive advantages in physical and chemical properties, including greatly enhanced fluorescence, higher catalytic activity, and better photon stability. Recent studies have found that adding Ag atoms to GNCs significantly increases fluorescence intensity, which may be attributed to their synergistic effect [163,164]. Due to the synergistic effect in enhancing NCs fluorescence, we seek to synthesize alloy NCs with enhanced fluorescence, such as Au–Ag NCs, to be considered as ideal fluorescent probes in FRET based biosensors. In addition, the large Stokes shift between the emission spectrum and excitation spectrum of Au–Ag NC reduces the reabsorption of the emitted light. Importantly, compared to QD or organic dyes, Au–Ag NC has lower toxicity and higher safety for living organisms. Li et al. [151] designed a fluorescent nanoprobe to determine L-Cys based on FRET from gold-silver bimetallic nanoclusters (Au–Ag NCs) to GNRs. Firstly, the negatively charged Au–Ag NCs protected by BSA were directly adsorbed on the surface of the positively charged GNRs through electrostatic interaction, and the FRET effect led to distinct fluorescence quenching of Au–Ag NCs at 615 nm. The introduction of L-Cys could effectively restore the fluorescence emission of the GNRs/Au–Ag NCs system. Under the optimized conditions, the fluorescence recovery of GNRs/Au–Ag NCs probe exhibited a linear response to L-Cys concentration ranging from 5 to 100 μM, and the corresponding theoretical LOD was 1.73 μM. Meanwhile, this method displays excellent sensitivity and selectivity for L-Cys over other amino acids, and it has been successfully applied to detect L-Cys in actual samples. The NIR MnCuInS/ZnS@BSA and urchin-like GNPs were designed [152] as a novel donor-acceptor pair for FRET assay by Xing H's group. As we know, gold nanoparticles (GNPs) are usually considered to be the star acceptors in FRET system due to their good stability, unfluctuating signal intensity, and strong resistance to photobleaching [165–167]. However, ordinary spherical GNPs usually do not absorb the NIR region light. Fortunately, except for ordinary spherical gold nanomaterials, different shapes of gold nanomaterials with different absorption spectra have also been studied, such as nanorod, nanocube, nanobipyramid, nanoplate and various branched NPs. In our strategy, urchin-like GNPs, a novel gold nanostructure, were selected as the energy acceptors. These ink blue urchin-like GNPs have advantages of abundant nanobridges, large specific surface area, improved uniformity and strong surface plasma absorption in NIR region. Based on their strong absorption in NIR region, fluorescence quenching efficiency can be enhanced and the signal sensitivity can be improved [18]. Their diameters are tunable and their peaks of surface plasmon resonance absorption can be adjusted to match exactly with the emission spectra of NIR QDs. By combining the optical advantages of NIR QDs encapsulated BSA nanoparticles with the excellent fluorescence quenching ability of urchin-like GNPs, the proposed FRET-based biosensor realized enhanced FRET effect for susceptible detection of human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2) in human serum samples. A wide detection range (2–100 ng/mL) and a low LOD (1 ng/mL) were obtained. This sensing system could decrease the interference of other biomolecules in NIR region, could be applied to further biomarker determination in vitro, and showed great potential for IVDs.

New composite nanomaterials or hybrid nanostructures based on GNPs can also be used to prepare fluorescent probes to improve detection sensitivity. Severe acute respiratory SARS-CoV-2 has dramatically changed the world and is a highly contagious virus. Timely and accurate diagnosis of SARS-CoV-2 infection is crucial for disease control and prevention. In this work, a fluorescence immunoassay was developed by Li and her groups to detect 2019-nCoV mAb [168]. GQDs and Ag@GNPs were successfully synthesized and characterized. FRET can effectively quench the fluorescence of GQD by Ag@GNPs [146]. Due to the existence of 2019-nCoV mAb, the steric hindrance between the Ag@GNPs-2019-nCoV antigen (NCP) complex and GQD was observed, which reduced the efficiency of FRET and restored the fluorescence of GQD. There was a satisfactory linear relationship between the fluorescence enhancement efficiency and the logarithmic concentration of 2019-nCoV mAb in the logarithmic range of 0.1 pg/mL - 10 ng/mL, and the LOD is 50 fg/mL. This method has good selectivity. When 2019-nCoV mAb was added to the serum sample, the recovery was between 90.8 % and 103.3 %. Fluorescent immunosensor showed the potential to supplement the existing serological tests for COVID-19 diagnosis. Because of the intense plasmonic light absorption and the corresponding FRET, the fluorescence quenching effect of Au tetrapod nanostars could be greatly enhanced by Ag coating. Moreover, the fluorescence quenching ability of Ag-coated Au tetrapod nanostars could be further increased by tuning the size of the inner Au tetrapod nanostars or the thickness of the outer Ag coating. In the study of CEA detection based on the fluorescence probe of Ag-coated Au tetrapod nanostars [144], surface modification of antibody conjugation has been carried out to guarantee specificity. Under the optimized conditions, the fluorescence efficiency of the Ag-coated Au tetrapod nanostars probe exhibited a linear response to CEA concentration ranging from 10 to 280 pg/mL, and the corresponding theoretical LOD is as low as 8.2 pg/mL. Upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) show great prospects in bioanalysis applications because of their excellent optical properties. In general, most analyses are based on the FRET principle to quench the fluorescence of UCNPs. However, each UCNP contains thousands of emission center ions, most exceeding the FRET critical distance, hindering FRET efficiency and leading to low S/N In this paper [145], a novel nanoprobe for the detection of xanthine (XA) based on internal filtering effect (IFE) and cascade signal amplification strategy was constructed by modifying UCNP with trypsin-chymotrypsin stabilized GNPs-gold nanoclusters (Try-chy-GNPs-GNCs). The Try-chy-GNPs-GNCs prepared by ultrafast (3 min) and green synthesis methods had efficient upconversion fluorescence quenching ability (quenching efficiency up to 90.9 %), which could effectively improve the SBR of the probe, thus improving the sensitivity. In addition, Try-chy-GNPs-GNCs had a unique spatial structure, which could effectively prevent the interaction between large-size biological mercaptan (GSH) and probe, thus improving its selectivity. In addition, combined with the excellent optical properties of UCNP and cascade signal amplification strategy, the sensitivity of the probe could be further improved. Under the optimized conditions, the linear response ranges of H2O2 and XA probes are 0.05–50 μM and 0.06–80 μM, and the detection limits are 22.6 nM and 26.3 nM respectively. At the same time, this method has been further applied to detecting XA in human serum.

The fluorescence quenching effect of gold nanomaterials is also widely used in immune detection. Various recognition elements such as polymers, antibodies, aptamers, biotins, and others have been functionalized onto the surface of GNPs to improve the specificity and stability of immune detection. Nucleic acid aptamers are a kind of single-stranded oligonucleotides screened artificially in vitro in recent years, which can efficiently and exclusively identify a variety of target substances. The fluorescent aptamer sensor detects the target substance by using the difference in fluorescence signal between the aptamer and target substance before and after the reaction. Antigen CD133 is a glycoprotein on the surface of cancer stem cells (CSCs). It is a key molecule to regulate the fate of stem cells and a functional marker of stem cells. Ding et al. [169] designed a novel fluorescent "on" nano-aptamer sensor for quantitative CD133, which was modified on the surface of QDs and GNPs respectively, by hybridization between CD133-targeted aptamers and partially complementary paired RNA (ssRNA). Due to the hybridization of nucleic acid aptamer and ssRNA, the distance between QDs and GNPs was shortened, resulting in FRET between them, and the fluorescence of QDs was quenched by GNPs. When CD133 competitively replaced ssRNA and bound to aptamers, GNssRNA can be released, which led to the recovery of fluorescence signals of QDs. The linear correlation between the relative value of fluorescence intensity and the concentration of CD133 in the range of 0–1.539 μM was studied, and the LOD was 6.99 nM. In the confocal images of A549 cells, the CD133 aptamer sensor was further proved suitable for lung cancer cell samples with specificity, precision. and accuracy. Compared with complex methods, this study provided a new method for developing CSC marker detection sensors with high sensitivity and selectivity. Based on the FRET between cadmium sulfide (CdS) QDs and GNPs, Li et al. [170] developed a closed fluorescence aptamer sensor to detect trace thrombin. Using DNA pairwise hybridization between nucleic acid aptamers and complementary DNA (cDNA), CdS QDs (energy donor) and GNPs (energy receptor) were closely coupled, leading to the occurrence of FRET and significant fluorescence quenching of CdS QDs (turn on). When thrombin was added to the fluorescent aptamer sensor, the aptamer bound specifically to the target to form a G-quadruplex, which led to the separation of GNPs receptors and the disassembly of DNA double strands. This process would inhibit FRET that contributes to fluorescence recovery (turn on) and construct an "off-on" fluorescent aptamer sensor for thrombin detection accordingly. Under the optimum conditions, the fluorescence recovery rate showed good linearity, the thrombin concentration was 1.35–54.0 nmol/L, and the LOD was 0.38 nmol/L (S/N = 3). Importantly, the fluorescent aptamer sensor has excellent specificity for thrombin and has been successfully applied to the quantitative determination of thrombin in real serum with a recovery rate of 98.6 %–102.2 %. Based on the FRET between peptide nucleic acid (PNA)-regulated CDs and GNPs, Gao et al. [171] established a convenient fluorimetric method for specific determination of DNA. In this system, CD with the maximum excitation/emission of lake blue fluorescence at 345/445 nm was used as the fluorescence reporter gene. In contrast, GNPs was used as the fluorescence nano-quenchant. A neutral PNA probe designed to identify target DNA was used as a coagulant to control the dispersion and aggregation of GNPs. No DNA, PNA can induce immediate GNPs aggregation, which leads to the recovery of quenching fluorescence emission of CD. However, complementary target DNA can protect GNPs from aggregation due to DNA/PNA complex formation, resulting in high fluorescence quenching efficiency of CD through dispersed GNPs. Under the optimized conditions, the quantitative evaluation of DNA is realized in the linear range of 5–100 nm, and the LOD is 0.21 nM. This method showed excellent specificity for perfectly matched DNA. In addition, applying this method in the sensitive determination of DNA in cell lysates proved its potential in bioanalysis and detection. Here, a new open fluorescence assay was established [172] as a platform for detecting transcription factor NF-kBp50 in the FRET system. This method was based on triple DNA labeled with N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) and GNPs as donors and receptors. Different characterization techniques studied the synthesized nanoparticles. A labeled DNA molecule was designed to form a triplet without a target protein, and changes in FRET efficiency reported its formation. When triple DNA was formed, the FRET between NCDs and GNPs quenched the fluorescence of 503 nm (460 nm excited) carbon points. However, with the presence of NF-kBp50, the fluorescence intensity caused by the release of GNPs-labeled single-stranded DNA from the triple DNA structure was significantly enhanced, which was used for sensitive determination of transcription factors(Fig. 12 ). This technique shows linearity in the range of 20–150 pM (R2 = 0.9943), and the LOD of NF-kBp50 is 9 pM. In addition, biosensors based on sequence-specific triple strands can selectively distinguish NF-kBp50 from other proteins. Our results showed that the biosensor provides a universal platform for the rapid detection of NF-kBp50 in a synthetic medium, which is helpful for the prevention and early diagnosis of cancer. In some diseases, such as cancer, disease progression is usually associated with the expression of multiple mRNAs. Therefore, multi-target simultaneous detection is of great significance to improve the accuracy of disease diagnosis.

The hairpin structure has been used to design polychromatic MBs, and Fan et al. [173] reported a FRET probe to detect three different tumor suppressor genes. The probe consisted of an GNPs core of 15 nm as a quenchant, and three hairpin sequences labeled with fluorescein, Texas Red, and Cy5 bind to NPs through the Au–S bond. The complementary target can attach to the ring sequence and open the stem ring structure like the traditional molecular beads. As a result, the dye was removed from the surface of GNPs, and three fluorescence signals were turned on simultaneously. Due to the different hairpin sequences on a single particle, these MBs have a great prospect in the multiple detection of single base mismatch DNA targets. This method can be easily extended to detect adenosine, potassium, and cocaine simultaneously by using their aptamers as hairpin sequences attached to GNPs quenchers.

Although the aptamer system of GNPs as a quenchant group is more focused on detecting biological macromolecules, there are also reports on the detection of some small molecular drugs and pathogens. Sulfamethazine (SMZ) exposed to the environment can enter the human body through the food chain, which poses a severe threat to human health. Wang et al. [174] developed an unlabeled fluorescence aptamer sensor based on specific aptamer (SMZ1S) and FRET between GNPs and rhodamine B (RhoB). In the absence of SMZ, SMZ1S was adsorbed on the surface of GNPs, causing GNPs to disperse in a high concentration salt solution, thus effectively quenching the fluorescence of RhoB. With the increase of SMZ concentration, the specific binding of SMZ1S and SMZ led to the aggregation of GNPs in the presence of NaCl, which reduced the fluorescence quenching of RhoB and increased the fluorescence intensity. Under the optimized conditions, the proposed fluorescence aptamer sensor showed a good linear detection effect in the binding buffer from 1.25 ng/mL to 40 ng/mL, and the detection limit was 0.82 ng/mL. The unlabeled fluorescent aptamer sensor studied in this study was a promising tool for detecting and quantifying SMZ in water and soil samples. Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) is a kind of pathogenic bacteria which seriously endangers food safety. Here, Ouyang et al. [175] developed a fast, sensitive, and specific aptamer sensor based on upconversion FRET for detecting S. aureus in food. The aptamer functionalized GNPs was combined with cDNA-modified upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs-cDNA) by complementary pairing, resulting in fluorescence quenching. After adding S. aureus to the system, the aptamer preferentially bound to S. aureus, isolated UCNPs-cDNA from GNPs aptamer, and recovered fluorescence. Under the optimized conditions, there was a significant linear correlation between fluorescence intensity and S. aureus concentration in the 47–4.7 × 10 7 CFU/mL (R2 = 0.9904), and the LOD was 10.7 CFU/mL. In addition, the standard plate counting method was used to verify the developed biosensors accuracy, and there was no significant difference. This method can be used to rapidly and sensitively quantify Staphylococcus aureus in food.

As an aptamer sensor model system, aptamer can also be combined into microfluidic devices, which have the advantages of low flow output, low consumption of detection materials, and regenerative performance [176]. Combined with the FRET detection technique on the micro/nanofluid preconcentrator, a rapid and sensitive protease activity determination has been developed in a uniform and real-time form. First of all, the dye-labeled proteins are loaded onto GNPs to form functional nanoprobes, in which the GNPs strongly quench photoluminescence of donor dyes due to the FRET mechanism. The nanoprobe was enriched by a micro/nanofluid preconcentrator to determine the protease activity. When the target protease is transported to the enriched nano-probe, the protein cleavage occurs due to the enzyme molecular recognition of the substrate. The release of cleavage fragments from the GNPs nanoprobe results in enhanced fluorescence and makes it possible to determine the protease activity on the micro/nanofluidic chip. As a demonstration, the digestion of fluorescein isothiocyanate labeled dog serum albumin (FITC-DSA) was digested by trypsin as a model reaction [149] (Fig. 13 a–b). It used Nafion membrane as the nanojunction to connect two V-shaped microchannels.A special substrate molecular recognition took place when the specific protease is transported to the preconcentrated nanoprobes. Due to the efficient pre-enrichment and spatial restriction effect, the protein cleavage rate and protease determination sensitivity can be significantly improved by enhancing enzyme activity (Fig. 13c–d). Results show that a trace level of trypsin as low as 1 ng/mL can be successfully assayed in 10 min. And reach a limit of detection of 0.1 ng/mL. The current micro/nanofluidic platform integrated with FRET detection technology is expected to be used for rapid and sensitive biological analysis, such as immunoassay, DNA hybridization, drug discovery, and clinical diagnosis.

As a new generation of fluorescent materials, fluorescent metal nanoclusters have become a concern to more and more researchers, especially GNCs, as the most typical representative of metal nanoclusters, have attracted more and more attention. GNCs is a molecular-level aggregate with fluorescence properties prepared using an organic single molecule as a template [177,178]. Its size is similar to that of Fermi wavelength (<2 nm) and can produce specific energy level separation to emit fluorescence under specific wavelength excitation. By virtue of one-photon excitation, two different emission wavelengths were found in the visible and NIR region. The energy diagram for nanoclusters shows that the emission in the visible region is most likely associated with the Au core, while that in the NIR region is attributed to the interaction of the surface ligands with GNCs [179]. Therefore, both the size of the Au core and the polarity of the ligand have a direct effect on the emission efficiency. It is reported that the emission of fluorescent GNCs can be gradually modulated from the ultraviolet region to NIR according to the size and composition of clusters. Compared with commonly used fluorescent materials such as organic fluorescence, QDs, and fluorescent proteins, GNCs have simple preparation methods, strong photoluminescence properties, and good biocompatibility. It has good light stability, a high release rate, and sub-nanometer size. The excellent functional combination makes gold nanoclusters show advantages and potential in some areas where traditional fluorescence is not competent. In the past few years, much work has been done synthesizing GNCs with high fluorescence quantum yield and its application in biomarkers and sensors. Here, we summarize some critical applications of GNC as fluorophores in biosensors and molecular diagnosis.

The ligands used to synthesize and stabilize GNCs are essential for determining the size and fluorescence intensity of GNCs because they tend to cluster into large clusters. It is reported that these ligands mainly include thiol-based small molecules, polymers, nucleotides, peptides, and proteins [180]. Mercaptan-protected gold nanoclusters are a new class of nanoparticles with ultra-small metal nuclei (<3 nm). Lei et al. established a new ratio fluorimetric method for the specific detection of folic acid (FA) based on fluorescence quenching using 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid (11-MUA)-bound GNCs. In this design [153], FA quenched the fluorescence of GNC@MUA at 612 nm by forming a ground state complex, while the autofluorescence of FA at 446 nm increased linearly with the titration of FA. Thus, the platform shows the double emission peaks at 446 nm and 612 nm excited by a single wavelength respectively, and the fluorescence intensity ratio I446/I612 can be used to detect FA.

BSA-encapsulated gold nanoclusters (GNC@BSA), due to their ultra-small size, unique molecular-like behavior, such as adjustable emission defined by the number of atoms forming nanoclusters [181], simplicity of one-pot synthesis, biocompatibility, negligible cytotoxicity [182], stability [183], and relatively long photoluminescence lifetime [184]. It has aroused considerable interest and demonstrated its application as a biosensor. The displaced GNC@BSA prepared by Fabijanic et al. [185] ultimately retained the binding of standard BSA ligands (small molecular drugs). Because of its red emission characteristics, the fluorescence detection ability was significantly improved in some cases. GNC@BSA effectively combined a series of aflatoxin-related mycotoxins and aliphatic mycotoxins FB1. 4 kinds of mycotoxins include sterigmatocystin (STC), 5-methoxysterigmatocystin (5-OME-STC), AFB1 and FB1 can interact with BSA at nanomole concentration in GNC@BSA. The binding constant of mycotoxin/GNC@BSA was determined by fluorescence quenching of GNC@BSA at 700 nm after 540 nm excitation. This red emission detection is superior to current detection strategies for the same mycotoxins, such as based on complex mass spectrometry procedures or eventually (after chemical modification of mycotoxins) through fluorescence detection in the UV range (<400 nm).

Like small organic fluorescent groups, forming GNCs aggregates can lead to fluorescence self-quenching. For example, Zhuo et al. [155] prepared magnetic fluorescent nanocomposites. It comprised Fe3O4 nanoparticles, a silica shell, and satellite GNCs coated with BSA. This kind of nanocomposites had many ideal properties, such as magnetism, red light emission, high water solubility, and high photobleaching resistance(Fig. 14 a). After adding the analyte 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP) or other similar mercaptan, because the interruption of the existing cross-linking bond in the structure of Fe3O4@SiO2 and GNCs was beneficial to the formation of gold-mercaptan bond, GNCs formed aggregates, which emitted red fluorescence at 613 nm under appropriate light conditions(Fig. 14b). The quenching ratio (F0–F)/F0 had a good linear relationship with the concentration of 6-MP in the range of 0.01–0.5 μM. The LOD was 0.004 M (S/N = 3). In the presence of oxidants, phenols, and heavy metal ions, especially biological mercaptan, this method had strong selectivity for 6-MP.

Cao et al. [186] developed a dual-wavelength digital imaging platform based on smartphones, which contained red (539–695 nm) and blue (389–511 nm) bandpass filters for POCT of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity. The platform was based on double emission fluorescence nanohybrid material (GNC@NaN), a ratio probe with an "on-off-on-off" fluorescence response. The probe uses red-emitting GNC as the signal reporting unit and blue-emitting N-(2-aminoethyl-1 (8-naphthalene dicarboxyimide) (NaN) as the internal reference. The different responses of the ratio metering probe led to a continuous multicolor change from pink to dark purple under the action of ALP. The dual-wavelength digital imaging platform was used to collect the image of GNCs or NaN fluorescence signal without the influence of background light. Unlike the classical one-time digital imaging mode, the accurate red (R) and blue (B) channel values of the generated image can help directly judge or eliminate the interference of inevitable interference factors. The sensor imaging platform successfully measured alkaline phosphatase activity in the range of 2.0–35.0 mU/mL, and the LOD was 1.04 mU/mL. The sensing imaging platform was used to determine alkaline phosphatase in human serum. The recovery rate was 94.9 %–105 %, and the relative standard deviation was 4.2 %–5.6 %.

GNCs-based fluorescence biological detection technology is also widely used in immune detection. Polymers, antibodies, aptamers, biotin, and other recognition elements have been functionalized on the surface of GNCs to improve the selectivity of immunoassays. Calreticulin (CRT) is cytoplasmic calcium-binding protein. In a study, Ramesh et al. [187] used succinic acid (MSA) as a coating GNC to study the cancer marker CRT. MSA as a functional coating has good dispersion, biological stability, biological coupling ability, and 800–850 nm fluorescence after broad band excitation. Within the range of ideal physiological pH, a specific anti-CRT antibody was linked to MSA to achieve the purpose of functional targeting and localization of biological imaging. In the model of colorectal cancer and breast cancer, the targeting of human umbilical vein endothelial cells was proved by antibody blocking; at the same time, a new way to make GNCs/MSA was shown, and it was proved that there was CRT on the surface of colon cancer and breast cancer cells. GNCs is used as a fluorescent biological probe to recognize surface-bound CRT specifically. These results are promising for applying GNCs in cancer treatment and CRT as a biomarker on the surface of human cancer.

Compared to traditional sensors that are time-consuming, require labeling, and require external primers, sensors that do not require labeling have higher application prospects. Moreover, with the increasing application of sensors in clinical settings, shortening clinical detection time is crucial for patients. Gold nanoparticles are not limited to DNA detection, but can also be used for detecting enzymes, biocatalysts, or glucose. This article mainly reviews the biological detectors based on gold nanochips, and summarizes the three main optical properties of this special material. At the same time, due to the easy modification property of gold nanoparticles, this type of biological detectors has a higher prospect, which has been demonstrated in many occasions. In summary, the application of gold nanochips in biosensors has been very extensive, and it will develop towards label free, high conductivity, and provide more accurate, fast, and specific services. At the same time, based on the previous description, it mainly implies the general development direction of in vitro diagnostics in order to adapt to technological development: (1) Using as few samples as possible can detect as many biomarkers or other indicators as possible; (2) Integrate the materials it needs to use and integrate them into a POC system; (3) Combine with machine learning such as recurrent neural networks and convolutional neural networks, connect deep learning-based algorithms with biosensors and introduce chemometrics into detection and diagnosis.

However, there are still some problems in the practical application of gold nanomaterials in vitro diagnosis, among which the main problem limiting the development of rapid immunoassay technology is its need for more sensitivity. To solve this problem, there are two main methods: (1) Improve the signal recognition system and analysis optimization; (2) Develop new tags or labeling systems. Several reviews have been published in related fields. Based on the excellent modification ability of gold nanomaterials, it has great application potential in in vitro diagnosis after ingenious use of its unique properties. At the same time, there are still many barriers to overcome to realize nanodiagnostic clinical transformation in vitro. The design, synthesis, surface modification, and biological coupling of nanomaterials are involved in the in vitro detection of nanomaterials, which are very important to determine the overall performance of the nanoprobes. It is vital to develop better preparation (especially microfabrication) and modification techniques to obtain nanoprobes with excellent repeatability, strong surface coating, and flexible functionalization and biological coupling processes. Still, at the same time, a stable and sensitive signal converter is also an important step that cannot be ignored. The commonly used signal conversion systems include electrochemical electrodes, thermistors, optics, piezoelectric crystals, mediators, or semiconductor materials. Most current nanodiagnostic techniques in vitro involve multiple steps, such as sample extraction, purification, assembly of sensors, and final detection, which require skilled personnel to complete.

Predictably, biosensors will be perfectly combined with artificial intelligence (AI), capable of automatically collecting and processing data, providing more scientific and accurate results, and achieving automated sampling, injection, and detection systems. At the same time, chip technology will increasingly enter the field of sensors to achieve integration and integration of detection systems. In addition, gold nanochips can not only be applied in the medical field, but also have matured in various aspects such as environmental detection and food safety. The development and combination of emerging technologies such as AI also provide new opportunities for this material. I hope this can contribute to the transformation of nanomaterial-based detection and serve society.
